topic: A student receives a much lower grade than expected
raw_output:
[story 1]

The fluorescent lights of the library hummed with an irritating monotony. Liam stared at the red ink bleeding across his paper, a stark testament to the disastrous outcome of the history essay. He tapped his pen against his lip, the rhythmic clicking sounding hollow in the vast silence. Another C-minus. It was just… another data point in a long, unremarkable sequence of academic disappointments. He sighed, a slow, deliberate release of air that did little to shift the heavy lethargy settling in his chest. He picked up a textbook, flipping through the pages with practiced indifference, the words swimming before his eyes like distant, meaningless shapes. Why bother trying to absorb this information when the reward felt so negligible? He traced the spine of the book, his gaze drifting toward the window where the afternoon sun cast long, uninteresting shadows across the quad.

[story 2]

"Seriously?" Maya muttered, the sound barely audible over the clatter of keyboards in the nearby study hall. She leaned back in her chair, the cheap plastic groaning under her weight. The feedback on the assignment screen glowed an unforgiving shade of crimson. A deep, internal slump occurred in her shoulders, a physical manifestation of utter lack of engagement. She picked up her lukewarm coffee, swirling the brown liquid mindlessly. The sheer effort it took to even formulate a coherent thought about the material felt monumental, an unnecessary expenditure of energy. She let her gaze drift to the ceiling tiles, watching a tiny dust mote dance in a stray beam of light. It was just… there. Nothing demanded her attention. She took a long, slow sip, the warmth doing nothing to ignite any spark of motivation.

[story 3]

Ethan drummed his fingers against the worn wooden desk, a rapid, uneven rhythm. The grade appeared on his screen, a figure that felt offensively low compared to the effort he'd poured in. He didn't flinch; he just stopped drumming, his hand falling still onto the surface. A profound sense of inertia washed over him, making even the act of breathing feel like a chore. He looked around the lecture hall, noticing the vacant expressions of his peers, the way they were either nodding along or staring blankly ahead. He felt utterly disconnected from the urgency of the lesson, as if the entire academic endeavor had suddenly become an elaborate, pointless performance. He leaned his head against the cool glass of the window, watching the indifferent flow of traffic outside, waiting for something, anything, to happen.
